attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old man presents to his General Practice clinic with a two-week history of worsening peri-umbilical pain. He reports intermittent umbilical discharge over the preceding months. He has no fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, or urinary symptoms. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears mildly uncomfortable. His vital signs are within normal limits with a temperature of 37.4°C. Abdominal examination reveals erythema in the peri-umbilical region (Image). There is mild tenderness, and warmth on palpation of the peri-umbilical region with focal rigidity within the umbilicus. There is no change in the size or consistency of the peri-umbilical area with coughing or abdominal wall tensing. He is overweight with a BMI of 34 kg/m2.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Omphalitis is the most likely diagnosis in this case. In adults, particularly those who are overweight, poor hygiene can lead to accumulation of debris in the umbilicus, forming concretions (omphaloliths) that can become infected. The patient's history of intermittent umbilical discharge, localised pain, and examination findings of erythema and tenderness without systemic symptoms are typical of omphalitis.

An obstructed paraumbilical hernia would typically present with a visible or palpable lump that changes with abdominal pressure, which is not described here. Additionally, there would be symptoms of obstruction including nausea, vomiting, and absence of flatus or bowel motions.

Acute haemorrhagic pancreatitis usually presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, vomiting, and often fever. The localised nature of this patient's symptoms and lack of systemic involvement make this diagnosis unlikely. If this pathology is present, it can cause periumbilical ecchymosis known as Cullen's sign.

A rectus sheath haematoma typically occurs after trauma or in patients on anticoagulation. It presents with acute onset of abdominal pain and a palpable mass that does not cross the midline. The gradual onset and lack of a distinct mass make this diagnosis less likely.

An infected urachal cyst is a possibility but less common than omphalitis. Urachal cysts are congenital remnants that can become infected, presenting with umbilical discharge and pain. However, they are relatively rare compared to simple omphalitis in adults.
learningPoints: Omphalitis in adults is often caused by poor hygiene and accumulation of debris in the umbilicus, particularly in overweight individuals.
Omphalitis typically presents with peri-umbilical pain, erythema, and possible discharge, without systemic symptoms.
Differential diagnosis of umbilical pain includes paraumbilical hernia, urachal cyst infection, and intra-abdominal pathologies.
references: Bosmann M, Schreiner O, Galle PR. Coexistence of Cullen's and Grey Turner's signs in acute pancreatitis. Am J Med. 2009;122(4):333-4. Available at: https://www.amjmed.com/article/S0002-9343(08)01059-0/abstract
Painter K, Anand S, Philip K. Omphalitis. In: StatPearls [Online]. 2022. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK513338/

Tawk A, Abdallah A, Meouchy P, Salameh J, Khoury S, Kyriakos M, et al. Omphalitis with Umbilical Abscess in an Adult with a Urachal Remnant. Case Rep Gastroenterol. 2021 14;15(3):966-971. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8739386/
